sentence_segments.text: In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step.
Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio.
Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane.
The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper.

The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively.
The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of R1 and R2, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends.
In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L.
The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well.

When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper).
The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible.
The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small.
When α is small, we have sinα ≈ tanα ≈ α, and cosα ≈ 1 - 1/2 α^2.
The magnitude of the velocity v of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates (x, y).
The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates (x, y).

Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates (x, y), when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

conditions.text: The working principle of a certain type of mass spectrometer is shown in Figure (1).

Region I is filled with a uniform magnetic field along the positive $x$-axis with magnetic field strength $B$, and region II is filled with a uniform electric field along the positive $x$-axis with electric field strength $E$.

A particle with mass $m$ and charge $+q$ drifts into the electric field from hole a (initial velocity considered zero), passes through hole $b$, enters the magnetic field, passes through point $c$ on plane $P$ (not drawn in the figure), and then enters the electric field before finally hitting the recording plate Q.
As shown in Figure (2), three points $s_1$, $s_2$, and $s_3$ are obtained on the recording plate.

targets.text: Determine the radius $R$ of the circular path of the particle in the magnetic field.

If these three points are the positions of a proton $_{1}^{1}\mathrm{H}$, a triton $_{1}^{3}\mathrm{H}$, and a helium nucleus $_{2}^{4}\mathrm{He}$, please specify which particle corresponds to each of these three points (neglecting the interaction between particles, no derivation required).

answer_slots.target_text: If these three points are the positions of a proton $_{1}^{1}\mathrm{H}$, a triton $_{1}^{3}\mathrm{H}$, and a helium nucleus $_{2}^{4}\mathrm{He}$, please specify which particle corresponds to each of these three points (neglecting the interaction between particles, no derivation required).
answer_slots.expected_answer:
$R=\frac{\sqrt{2m q U}}{q B}$
$L=\frac{\sqrt{2m q U}}{q B}-\sqrt{\frac{2m U}{q B^{2}}-d^{2}}$
$x=\frac{m d^{2}E}{4m U-2q d^{2}B^{2}}$
$y=R-\sqrt{R^{2}-d^{2}}+\frac{d^{2}}{\sqrt{R^{2}-d^{2}}}$
$s_1$, $s_2$, and $s_3$ correspond to the positions of the tritium nucleus $_{1}^{3}\mathrm{H}$, the helium nucleus $_{2}^{4}\mathrm{He}$, and the proton $_{1}^{1}\mathrm{H}$, respectively.

sentence_segments.text: When studying the structure of atomic nuclei, it is necessary to bombard the nuclei with particles of very high energy.
To enable charged particles to attain such high energies, scientists have invented various types of particle accelerators.

Figure 1 shows a schematic diagram of an accelerating device, which consists of multiple metal cylinders with the same cross-sectional area arranged in sequence, with their axes aligned on the same straight line.
The cylinders with odd numbers are connected to one pole of an alternating current (AC) power supply, while those with even numbers are connected to the other pole.
The variation pattern of the potential difference between the two poles of the AC power supply is shown in Figure 2.
At $t = 0$, the potential difference between the odd-numbered cylinders and the even-numbered cylinders is positive.
At this moment, an electron is located at the center of a metal plate (numbered 0) connected to the even-numbered cylinders.
The electron starts from rest and is accelerated by the electric field between the plate and cylinder 1, entering cylinder 1 along the central axis.
To ensure that the electron is accelerated in the gaps between the cylinders, the lengths of the cylinders must be designed according to a specific rule.

If the mass of the electron is $m$, its charge is $-e$, the voltage of the AC power supply is $U$, and the period is $T$, the electric field in the gap between two cylinders can be considered as a uniform electric field, while the electric field inside the cylinders is zero.
The gravitational force and relativistic effects on the electron are neglected.

Determine the velocity $\mathbf{v}_{1}$ of the electron as it enters cylinder 1, and analyze the motion of the electron from the moment it starts moving from the circular plate until it exits cylinder 2.
If the time for electrons to pass through the gaps between cylinders is neglected, what should be the length $\mathrm{L}_{\mathrm{n}}$ of the nth metal cylinder?

If the time for electrons to pass through the cylindrical gap cannot be neglected, and the distance of the cylindrical gap is $d$, while maintaining the same cylindrical length, the variation law of the alternating voltage, and the conditions in (2), what is the maximum speed that the device can allow the electrons to attain?

conditions.text: Supercapacitors are high-capacity capacitors with a capacitance $C$ reaching thousands or even tens of thousands of farads, offering advantages such as high power density, fast charging speed, and long cycle life.
The circuit shown in the diagram is used to charge a supercapacitor.

The charging process is divided into two stages: the first stage is constant current charging (i.e., the current output by the charger remains unchanged), and when the charger detects that the voltage reaches a certain value, it enters the second stage, where constant voltage charging (i.e., the voltage output by the charger remains unchanged) is performed until charging is terminated.
When entering the second stage for constant voltage charging, the voltage output by the charger is $U$, and at a certain moment, the current flowing through the resistor $R$ is ${I}_{2}$.

targets.text: When it is charged with a constant current $I$ in the first stage, find the voltage $U_1$ across the capacitor at time $t$.
Find the voltage $U_2$ across the capacitor.

answer_slots.target_text: Find the voltage $U_2$ across the capacitor.

sentence_segments.text: Supercapacitors are high-capacity capacitors with a capacitance $C$ reaching thousands or even tens of thousands of farads, offering advantages such as high power density, fast charging speed, and long cycle life.

The charger has the capability to control the output voltage and output current.

The charging process is divided into two stages: the first stage is constant current charging (i.e., the current output by the charger remains unchanged), and when the charger detects that the voltage reaches a certain value, it enters the second stage, where constant voltage charging (i.e., the voltage output by the charger remains unchanged) is performed until charging is terminated.
The resistance value is constant at $R$.
The capacitor is initially uncharged with a capacitance of $C$.
When it is charged with a constant current $I$ in the first stage, find the voltage $U_1$ across the capacitor at time $t$.

The conveyor belt with a wheel radius of $R=0.4\mathsf{m}$ and a shaft distance of $L=2\mathsf{m}$ rotates counterclockwise at a constant linear velocity.
The lowest point of the wheel is at a height of $H=2.2m$ above the ground.

Assume that the magnitude of the velocity of the small block remains unchanged when it moves from end B of the straight track to point C on the conveyor belt, but the direction changes to horizontal and rightward.
It is known that the coefficient of kinetic friction between the small block and the straight track and the conveyor belt is $\mu=0.5$.

targets.text: If $h=2.4m$, calculate the magnitude of the velocity of the small block when it reaches point B.
If the small block falls to the ground on the left side of the conveyor belt, determine the condition that h must satisfy.
Find the relationship between the horizontal distance $x$ from the landing point of the small block to point D and h, and also determine the condition that h must satisfy.

answer_slots.target_text: Find the relationship between the horizontal distance $x$ from the landing point of the small block to point D and h, and also determine the condition that h must satisfy.

sentence_segments.text: To improve transportation efficiency, a sand yard studies the relationship between the height of sand particle descent and the motion of sand particles on a conveyor belt, establishing a physical model as shown in the figure.
There is a straight track AB with an inclination angle of $\theta=37^{\circ}$ in the vertical plane.
A horizontal conveyor belt is placed below and to the right of it.
The distance between the end B of the straight track and the conveyor belt can be approximated as zero, but allows sand particles to pass through.
The conveyor belt with a wheel radius of $R=0.4\mathsf{m}$ and a shaft distance of $L=2\mathsf{m}$ rotates counterclockwise at a constant linear velocity.
The lowest point of the wheel is at a height of $H=2.2m$ above the ground.
Now, a small block is released from rest at a height h above the conveyor belt.

Assume that the magnitude of the velocity of the small block remains unchanged when it moves from end B of the straight track to point C on the conveyor belt, but the direction changes to horizontal and rightward.
It is known that the coefficient of kinetic friction between the small block and the straight track and the conveyor belt is $\mu=0.5$.
If $h=2.4m$, calculate the magnitude of the velocity of the small block when it reaches point B.
If the small block falls to the ground on the left side of the conveyor belt, determine the condition that h must satisfy.
By changing the release height h of the small block, the small block is projected horizontally to the right from point D on the conveyor belt.
Find the relationship between the horizontal distance $x$ from the landing point of the small block to point D and h, and also determine the condition that h must satisfy.

conditions.text: A small ball of mass $m = 0.1\,\mathrm{kg}$ is thrown vertically upwards with an initial velocity of $v_0 = 10\,\mathrm{m/s}$ and is caught at the launch point.

conditions.text: As depicted in the figure, a fixed smooth quarter-circular arc track has a radius of $R=0.2\mathrm{m}$.

targets.text: What is the pressure exerted by B on A when B slides to the bottom of A?
If B does not collide with the barrier at the right end of C, what is the amount of heat generated by friction between B and C when B is at rest relative to the ground?
Calculate the time it takes for B to come to a complete stop after sliding onto C.

conditions.text: As shown in figure (a), there are two small sliders A and B, each with a mass of $m$, on a rough, insulated horizontal plane.
Their charges are $q$ ($q>0$) and $-q$, respectively.
A uniform electric field with a magnitude of $E$ is present throughout the space, directed horizontally to the right.
The maximum static friction between A, B and the horizontal plane is equal to the sliding friction, both with a magnitude of $2qE$.
At $t=0$, A moves to the right with an initial velocity of $v_0$, while B is at rest.
At time $t_1$, A reaches position S with a velocity of $v_1$, and the spring is not yet in contact with B.
At time $t_2$, the velocity of A reaches its maximum, and the magnitude of the spring force at this time is $3qE$.
The $v$-$t$ graph of A in the time interval $0\sim t_3$ is shown in figure (b), where $v_1$ is the minimum speed in the graph, and $t_1$, $t_2$, and $t_3$ are all unknowns.

Calculate the total work done by the uniform electric field on A and B during the time interval $t_1\sim t_2$.
If the initial velocity of A is increased such that its velocity at position S is $2v_1$, determine the velocity of A at the instant before the rod collides with B.

answer_slots.target_text: If the initial velocity of A is increased such that its velocity at position S is $2v_1$, determine the velocity of A at the instant before the rod collides with B.

During the motion, A and B are on the same straight line, and the charges of A and B remain constant.
The Coulomb force between them is equivalent to the electrostatic force between point charges in a vacuum, with the electrostatic constant being $k$.

conditions.text: A local region of a Hall thruster can be abstracted into a model as shown in the figure.

conditions.text: As shown in Figure 1, a rigid conductive wire frame consists of two vertical rods of length $L$ and mass $m$ each, and two light horizontal rods of length $2l$, where $L>>2l$.

$\frac{2B_{0}S}{R}$
$I=\{\begin{array}{c l}{0, \quad 0 \leq t \leq \frac{\pi}{3\omega}} {-\frac{6\omega B_{0}S}{\pi R} \quad \frac{\pi}{3\omega}< t \leq \frac{2\pi}{3\omega}}\end{array}$
$\frac{2\sqrt{3}\omega B_{0}S}{\pi R}$
$\frac{6{B_{0}}^{2}{S}^{2}}{\pi k m l^{2}R}$

sentence_segments.text: A spot galvanometer is an instrument that can measure minute currents, and its simplified working principle diagram is shown in the figure.
In the figure, A is a lightweight insulating spring, C is a coil located on the plane of the paper, and there is a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the paper within the dashed box

M is a lightweight small planar mirror placed on a platform, one end of the lightweight rigid thin rod D is fixed to M and perpendicular to the mirror surface, and the other end is connected to the lower end of the spring.
PQ is a circular arc-shaped transparent reading strip with uniform graduations, and the center of the circle of PQ is located at the center of M.
Zeroing is required before use: when no current flows through the coil, M is vertical and perpendicular to the paper
an incident thin light beam is directed horizontally through point O on PQ to M and then reflected back along the original path.
After a current is passed through the coil, the length of the spring changes, causing M to tilt.
The incident point of the incident light beam on M is still approximately at the center of PQ.
By reading the position where the reflected light hits PQ, the magnitude of the current can be measured.

The spring constant is k, the magnitude of the magnetic field induction is B, the number of turns of coil C is N, the length along the horizontal direction is l, the length of thin rod D is d, and the radius of arc PQ is r.
d is much larger than the absolute value of the change in spring length.
If a minute current I is passed through the coil, find the absolute value of the change in spring length Δx after equilibrium is reached.
Calculate the arc length s between the reflected light spot and point O on PQ.
A student uses this device to measure a minute current.
Before the measurement, the device is not zeroed.
After passing the current through the coil, the reflected light spot on PQ appears above point O, and the arc length between it and point O is s1.
Keeping other conditions unchanged, only the direction of the current is reversed, and the reflected light spot appears below point O, and the arc length between it and point O is s2.
Calculate the magnitude of the current to be measured.

conditions.text: As shown in the figure, in the apparatus for studying the photoelectric effect, metal plates M and N are placed parallel to the y-axis, with a small hole O located at the center of plate N.
Region I is bounded by the x-axis, y-axis, and two circular arcs with different radii, centered at O with a central angle of 90°.

This region contains a uniform electric field with adjustable magnitude, directed into the page, and a magnetic field with a constant magnetic induction of B1, whose magnetic field lines are parallel to the circular arcs and directed counterclockwise.
To the right of Region I, there is a uniform magnetic field Region II, whose left boundary is parallel to the y-axis and a distance I away from it, and its lower boundary coincides with the x-axis.
After photoelectrons are emitted from plate M, they are accelerated by the voltage U between the plates.

By adjusting the electric field strength in Region I and the magnetic induction in Region II, the electrons are precisely directed to hit the point with coordinates (a+2I, 0), where a detector is placed.
The electron mass is m, the electron charge is e, the work function of plate M is W0, and the Planck constant is h.

To ensure that all electrons, regardless of their magnitude and direction of velocity, that enter Region I from point O are detected, the electric field strength E in Region I and the magnetic induction B2 in Region II need to be adjusted.

targets.text: Determine the maximum initial kinetic energy Ekm of the emitted photoelectrons.

Determine the maximum values of E and B2.

conditions.text: A horizontal metal ring with a radius of $r=0.2\mathsf{m}$ is fixed.
Two metal rods, each with a length of $r$ and a resistance of $R_{0}$, are placed along the diameter of the ring.
The rods rotate uniformly with an angular velocity of $\omega=600\ \mathrm{rad/s}$.
A uniform magnetic field with magnetic induction strength $B_{1}$ exists in the left half of the ring.
The edge of the ring and the brushes in good contact with the rotating shaft are connected to parallel horizontal metal tracks with a distance of $l_{1}$.
A capacitor with capacitance $C=0.09\ \mathrm{F}$ is connected between the tracks.
The capacitor can be connected to terminals 1 and 2 via a single-pole double-throw switch $S$.
On the left side of the capacitor is a region with width $l_{1}$, length $l_{2}$, and a uniform magnetic field with magnetic induction strength $B_{2}$.
Outside the magnetic field region, there are insulated tracks with a distance of $l_{1}$ smoothly connected to the metal tracks.
The length of rod $ab$ and the width of the "["-shaped frame are both $l_{1}$, and their masses are both $m=0.01\ \mathrm{kg}$.
The lengths of $de$ and $cf$ are both $l_{3}=0.08\ \mathrm{m}$.
It is known that $l_{1}=0.25\ \mathrm{m}$, $l_{2}=0.068\ \mathrm{m}$, $B_{1}=B_{2}=1\ \mathrm{T}$, and both magnetic fields are vertically upwards.
The resistance of rod $ab$ and side $cd$ of the "["-shaped frame are both $R=0.1\ \Omega$.
Initially, switch $S$ is connected to terminal 1.
After the capacitor is fully charged, $S$ is switched from 1 to 2.
At this point, $S$ is disconnected from 2.
It is known that the center of mass of frame $abcd$ rises by $0.2\ \mathrm{m}$ on the inclined track before returning into the magnetic field.

targets.text: Determine the charge $Q$ on the capacitor after it is fully charged, and identify which plate ($M$ or $N$) is positively charged.

conditions.text: In a cyclotron, a uniform circular magnetic field region is centered at point $O$, with a magnetic field strength of $B$.
These particles drift into the accelerating electric field near $O$, and after multiple accelerations, they move in a circular path around $O$ with a radius of $R$, passing through point $P$.
To extract the particles from the magnetic field, a "static deflector" is installed at position $P$, as shown in Figure b.
The deflector's two electrodes, $M$ and $N$, have uniform thickness and form a circular arc-shaped slit.
When a voltage is applied between $M$ and $N$, an electric field with a strength of $E$ is produced within the slit, allowing the particle to pass through it.

targets.text: What is the time $t$ required for the particle to be accelerated to point $P$?
What is the maximum thickness $d_{\mathrm{m}}$ of the electrode $N$?
What is the maximum radius $R_{\mathrm{m}}$ of the magnetic field region?

answer_slots.target_text: What is the maximum radius $R_{\mathrm{m}}$ of the magnetic field region?
answer_slots.expected_answer:
$t=\frac{q B^{2}R^{2}}{2m U}-1)\frac{\pi m}{q B}$
$d_{\mathrm{m}} = 2(\sqrt{R^{2}-\frac{2m U}{q B^{2}}}-\sqrt{R^{2}-\frac{4m U}{q B^{2}}})$
$R_{\mathrm{m}} = R+\frac{2m E R}{q B^{2}R-m E}\sin\frac{\alpha}{2}$

conditions.text: A coordinate system $xyz$ is established using the three sides of the rectangular parallelepiped as axes, as shown in the figure.

When a constant current is applied along the $+x$ direction through the semiconductor material, a uniform magnetic field with a magnetic flux density of magnitude $B$ is applied along the $+y$ direction at a certain moment.
After the Hall electric field is established, the directional drift velocities of free electrons and holes in the $z$ direction are $v_{nz}$ and $v_{pz}$, respectively.

targets.text: Determine the direction of the Lorentz force on a free electron immediately after the magnetic field is applied.

If the current formed by the directional movement of free electrons along the $+x$ direction is $I_n$, determine the magnitude $F_{nz}$ of the net force, which is the resultant of the Lorentz force and the Hall electric force, acting on a single free electron in the $z$ direction due to its directional motion.

Determine the number of free electrons and holes that move to the top surface of the semiconductor in the $z$ direction within a time interval $\Delta t$, and state the condition that the currents formed by the two types of charge carriers in the $z$ direction should satisfy.

answer_slots.target_text: Determine the number of free electrons and holes that move to the top surface of the semiconductor in the $z$ direction within a time interval $\Delta t$, and state the condition that the currents formed by the two types of charge carriers in the $z$ direction should satisfy.
answer_slots.expected_answer:
The free electron experiences a Lorentz force along the $+z$ direction.
$F_{nz}=e\left(\frac{I_n B}{n e a b}+E\right)$
$N_n = n a c v_{nz} \, \Delta t$, $N_p = p a c v_{pz} \, \Delta t$, $N_n = N_p$; the currents formed by the two types of carriers in the $z$ direction should have equal magnitudes and opposite directions.

sentence_segments.text: A Hall element is an important magnetic sensor used in various automatic control systems.
A rectangular parallelepiped semiconductor material has a thickness of $a$, a width of $b$, and a length of $c$.

There are two types of charge carriers in the semiconductor: free electrons and holes, both with a charge magnitude of $e$.
Holes can be regarded as free-moving particles with a positive charge.
The number of free electrons and holes per unit volume are $n$ and $p$, respectively.

When a constant current is applied along the $+x$ direction through the semiconductor material, a uniform magnetic field with a magnetic flux density of magnitude $B$ is applied along the $+y$ direction at a certain moment.
Consequently, a stable electric field is quickly established in the $z$ direction, known as the Hall electric field.
The magnitude of the electric field strength is $E$, and it is along the $-z$ direction.
Determine the direction of the Lorentz force on a free electron immediately after the magnetic field is applied.

If the current formed by the directional movement of free electrons along the $+x$ direction is $I_n$, determine the magnitude $F_{nz}$ of the net force, which is the resultant of the Lorentz force and the Hall electric force, acting on a single free electron in the $z$ direction due to its directional motion.
After the Hall electric field is established, the directional drift velocities of free electrons and holes in the $z$ direction are $v_{nz}$ and $v_{pz}$, respectively.

Determine the number of free electrons and holes that move to the top surface of the semiconductor in the $z$ direction within a time interval $\Delta t$, and state the condition that the currents formed by the two types of charge carriers in the $z$ direction should satisfy.

conditions.text: The simplified principle is shown in the figure.
It consists of two vertical guide rails, a rocket-carrying device (simplified as an insulated conducting rod MN), and a device $A$, forming a closed circuit.
Device $A$ can automatically adjust its output voltage to ensure a constant current $I$ in the circuit, with the direction as shown.

At the location of the rod, the magnetic field is approximately uniform, with magnitude $B_{1}=kI$ (where $k$ is a constant), and the direction is perpendicular inward to the plane of the guide rails.
The magnetic field generated between the two guide rails below the conducting rod is approximately uniform, with magnitude $B_{2}=2kI$, and the direction is the same as $B_{1}$.
When the rocket descends without power to the top of the guide rails, it adheres to the conducting rod and enters the guide rails with a velocity $v_{0}$.
It is known that the total mass of the rocket and the conducting rod is $M$, the distance between the guide rails is $d=\frac{3Mg}{kI^{2}}$, and the resistance of the conducting rod is $R$.

targets.text: Determine the magnitude of the A force $F$ experienced by the conducting rod.
Determine the distance $L$ traveled by the conducting rod.
Determine the relationship between the induced electromotive force $E$ in the circuit and the time $t$.
Determine the relationship between the output voltage $U$ of device $A$ and the time $t$.
Determine the energy $W$ output by device $A$.
If the resistance $R$ is taken to be 0, and device $A$ is used for energy recovery, state the source and magnitude of the energy that device $A$ can recover.

answer_slots.target_text: If the resistance $R$ is taken to be 0, and device $A$ is used for energy recovery, state the source and magnitude of the energy that device $A$ can recover.
answer_slots.expected_answer:
1. $F=3Mg$
2. $L=\frac{v_{0}^{2}}{4g}$
3. $E=\frac{6Mg}{I}(v_{0}-2gt)$
4. $U=IR-\frac{6Mg}{I}(v_{0}-2gt)$
5. $W=\frac{I^{2}Rv_{0}}{2g}-\frac{3Mv_{0}^{2}}{2}$
6. Device $A$ can recover the rocket's kinetic energy and gravitational potential energy; the recovered energy is $\frac{3}{2}Mv_{0}^{2}$.

sentence_segments.text: An interest group designed a rocket landing device.

The magnetic field generated between the two guide rails below the conducting rod is approximately uniform, with magnitude $B_{2}=2kI$, and the direction is the same as $B_{1}$.
When the rocket descends without power to the top of the guide rails, it adheres to the conducting rod and enters the guide rails with a velocity $v_{0}$.
Upon reaching the insulated stopping platform, the velocity is exactly zero, completing the rocket landing process.
It is known that the total mass of the rocket and the conducting rod is $M$, the distance between the guide rails is $d=\frac{3Mg}{kI^{2}}$, and the resistance of the conducting rod is $R$.
The conducting rod maintains good contact with the guide rails during sliding.
Air resistance and friction are neglected, and the resistance of the guide rails and the internal resistance of device $A$ are also neglected.
Determine the magnitude of the A force $F$ experienced by the conducting rod.
Determine the distance $L$ traveled by the conducting rod.
Determine the relationship between the induced electromotive force $E$ in the circuit and the time $t$.
Determine the relationship between the output voltage $U$ of device $A$ and the time $t$.
Determine the energy $W$ output by device $A$.
If the resistance $R$ is taken to be 0, and device $A$ is used for energy recovery, state the source and magnitude of the energy that device $A$ can recover.

sentence_segments.text: As shown in the figure, $M$ is a particle accelerator
$N$ is a velocity selector, with a uniform electric field and a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to each other between two parallel conductive plates.
The magnetic field direction is into the page, with a magnetic flux density of $B$.

A positively charged particle with mass $m$ and charge $q$, released from point $S$ with an initial velocity of 0, is accelerated by $M$ and then passes through $N$ along a straight line (the dashed line parallel to the conductive plates) with velocity $v$.
Gravity is negligible.
Determine the accelerating voltage $U$ of the particle accelerator $M$.
Determine the magnitude and direction of the electric field strength $E$ between the two plates of the velocity selector $N$.

Another positively charged particle with mass $2m$ and charge $q$, also released from point $S$ with an initial velocity of 0, departs from $N$ with a deviation of $d$ from the dashed line in the figure.
Calculate the kinetic energy $E_k$ of this particle when it leaves $N$.

conditions.text: A uniform electric field with a field strength of $E$ is present between plates M and N, directed along the positive $z$-axis.
The components along the $x$ and $y$ directions, $B_x$ and $B_y$, vary periodically with time, as shown in Figure (2), where $B_0$ is adjustable.
A beam of xenon ions ($Xe^{2+}$) is emitted from the small hole $S$ of the ion source and moves at a uniform velocity along the $z$ direction towards plate M.
After being accelerated by the electric field, the ions enter the magnetic field region and finally exit from the end surface $P$.
The velocity of the ions after being accelerated by the electric field at the center point $O$ of metal plate N relative to the thruster is measured to be $v_0$.
The mass of a single ion is $m$, and the charge is $2e$.

Neglecting the ions' motion during the abrupt changes in the magnetic field, adjust the value of $B_0$ such that all the ions emitted from the small hole $S$ can exit from the rear surface $P$ of the nozzle.
The number of ions emitted from the end surface $P$ per unit time is $n$, and $B_0=\frac{\sqrt{2}mv_0}{5eL}$.

targets.text: Determine the magnitude of the velocity $v_s$ of the ions relative to the thruster when they are emitted from the small hole $S$.
Determine the range of values for $B_0$.
Determine the component of the force exerted by the ion beam on the thruster along the z-axis direction at time $t_0$ in Figure (2).

answer_slots.target_text: Determine the component of the force exerted by the ion beam on the thruster along the z-axis direction at time $t_0$ in Figure (2).

sentence_segments.text: A certain ion thruster on a space station consists of an ion source, two parallel metal plates M and N with a small hole and a separation of $d$, and a cube with side length $L$.
The rear surface $P$ of the cube serves as the nozzle.
A coordinate system is established with the center $O$ of metal plate N as the origin, with the $x$, $y$, and $z$ axes perpendicular to the sides of the cube and the metal plates.
A uniform electric field with a field strength of $E$ is present between plates M and N, directed along the positive $z$-axis.
Inside the cube, a magnetic field exists, whose magnetic induction intensity component along the $z$ direction is always zero.
The components along the $x$ and $y$ directions, $B_x$ and $B_y$, vary periodically with time, as shown in Figure (2), where $B_0$ is adjustable.
A beam of xenon ions ($Xe^{2+}$) is emitted from the small hole $S$ of the ion source and moves at a uniform velocity along the $z$ direction towards plate M.
After being accelerated by the electric field, the ions enter the magnetic field region and finally exit from the end surface $P$.
The velocity of the ions after being accelerated by the electric field at the center point $O$ of metal plate N relative to the thruster is measured to be $v_0$.
The mass of a single ion is $m$, and the charge is $2e$.
The interactions between ions are negligible, and the total mass of emitted ions is much less than the mass of the thruster.
Determine the magnitude of the velocity $v_s$ of the ions relative to the thruster when they are emitted from the small hole $S$.

Neglecting the ions' motion during the abrupt changes in the magnetic field, adjust the value of $B_0$ such that all the ions emitted from the small hole $S$ can exit from the rear surface $P$ of the nozzle.
Determine the range of values for $B_0$.
Assume the time the ions spend in the magnetic field is much smaller than the period $T$ of the magnetic field variation.
The number of ions emitted from the end surface $P$ per unit time is $n$, and $B_0=\frac{\sqrt{2}mv_0}{5eL}$.
Determine the component of the force exerted by the ion beam on the thruster along the z-axis direction at time $t_0$ in Figure (2).

conditions.text: As shown in Figure (1), a device for detecting gas discharge processes has two electrodes connected to a long straight wire in an ionization chamber filled with neon gas (Ne).

In a plane perpendicular to the long straight wire, a circular solenoid made of thin wire with a resistance of $R_{0}=10\Omega$ and $N=5\times 10^{3}$ turns is installed, with the axis of symmetry aligned with the straight wire.
The two ends, c and d, of the thin wire are connected to a resistor of resistance $R=90\Omega$.
The cross-section of the solenoid is a circle with a radius of $a=1.0\times10^{-2}\mathrm{m}$, and its center is at a distance of $r=0.1\mathrm{m}$ from the long straight wire.
After the gas is ionized, a clockwise current $I$ is generated in the long straight wire circuit, and its $I-t$ graph is shown in Figure (2).

For the convenience of calculation, the magnitude of the magnetic induction intensity at all locations inside the solenoid can be regarded as $B={\frac{k I}{r}}$, where $k=2\times10^{-7}\mathrm{T\cdot m/A}$.

targets.text: Determine the amount of charge $Q$ that passes through the cross-section of the long straight wire within the time interval of $0 \sim 6.0\times10^{-3}\mathrm{s}$.
Calculate the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through one turn of the solenoid at time $3.0\times10^{-3}\mathrm{s}$.

answer_slots.target_text: Calculate the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through one turn of the solenoid at time $3.0\times10^{-3}\mathrm{s}$.

The cross-section of the solenoid is a circle with a radius of $a=1.0\times10^{-2}\mathrm{m}$, and its center is at a distance of $r=0.1\mathrm{m}$ from the long straight wire.

Calculate the magnetic flux $\Phi$ through one turn of the solenoid at time $3.0\times10^{-3}\mathrm{s}$.

conditions.text: As shown in the figure, two baffles, each with a length of $l$, are placed vertically opposite each other, with a spacing of $l$.
Above this horizontal level, there is a uniform electric field directed vertically downwards with an electric field strength of magnitude $E$.
Between the two baffles, there is a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the paper and outwards, with an adjustable magnetic induction strength.

The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively.

targets.text: Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio.

sentence_segments.text: In the process of chip manufacturing, ion implantation is a crucial step.
Ions are accelerated and enter a velocity selector horizontally, then pass through a magnetic analyzer, which selects ions with a specific charge-to-mass ratio.
Subsequently, the ions are deflected by a deflection system and implanted into a wafer (silicon chip) lying in the horizontal plane.
The uniform magnetic fields in the velocity selector, magnetic analyzer, and deflection system all have a magnetic induction magnitude of B, directed outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper.

The uniform electric fields in the velocity selector and deflection system have an electric field magnitude of E, directed vertically upwards and outward perpendicular to the plane of the paper, respectively.

The cross-section of the magnetic analyzer is a quarter-circular ring with inner and outer radii of $R_{1}$ and $R_{2}$, respectively, with small apertures at the center positions M and N at its ends.
In the deflection system, the electric and magnetic fields are distributed within the same cube with side length L.
The bottom of the deflection system is parallel to the horizontal plane of the wafer, and the distance between them is L as well.

When no electric or magnetic field is applied to the deflection system, the ions are injected vertically onto point O on the wafer (which is the origin of coordinates in the figure, with the x-axis directed outward perpendicular to the paper).
The entire system is in a vacuum, and the gravitational force on ions is negligible.
The deflection angles of ions caused by the electric and magnetic fields are both small.
When $\alpha$ is small, we have $\sin\alpha \approx \tan\alpha \approx \alpha$, and $\cos \alpha \approx 1-\frac{1}{2}\alpha^{2}$.
The magnitude of the velocity $v$ of the ions after passing through the velocity selector and the charge-to-mass ratio of the ions selected by the magnetic analyzer.
The position of the ion-implanted wafer, when only an electric field is applied in the deflection system, is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.
The position where ions are implanted on the wafer when only a magnetic field is applied in the deflection system is represented by coordinates $(x, y)$.

Determine the position where the ions are implanted on the wafer, represented by coordinates $(x, y)$, when both electric and magnetic fields are applied simultaneously in the deflection system, and provide the justification.

conditions.text: The successful lunar landing and return of Chang'e-5 is inspiring.

The entire device has a velocity of magnitude $v_{0}$ immediately before landing vertically on the lunar surface.

After deceleration, the cabin can be considered to be moving at a constant speed before the buffer spring below the rails contacts the lunar surface.

The gravitational acceleration on the lunar surface is $\frac{g}{6}$.

The side ab of the ∧-shaped wire frame can slide along the rails while maintaining good contact.
The cabin, rails, and magnet are fixed together, with a total mass of $m_{1}$.
The entire device has a velocity of magnitude $v_{0}$ immediately before landing vertically on the lunar surface.
The velocity of the wire frame becomes zero immediately after making contact with the lunar surface.

sentence_segments.text: The Tiangong space station utilizes Hall thrusters for attitude control and orbit correction.

additionally, there is a radial magnetic field pointing outward with uniform magnitude everywhere.

The numerous electrons inside the discharge chamber can be considered to be located near the anode, performing uniform circular motion with a radius $R$ around the axis in a plane perpendicular to the axis (as shown in the cross-sectional view), which can collide with the xenon atoms injected from the left end and ionize them.
Each xenon ion has a mass $M$ and a charge $+e$, with an initial velocity approximately zero.

The xenon ions are accelerated by the electric field and eventually ejected from the right end of the discharge chamber, precisely neutralizing the electrons emitted by the cathode that did not enter the discharge chamber.

conditions.text: When studying the structure of atomic nuclei, it is necessary to bombard the nuclei with particles of very high energy.
The variation pattern of the potential difference between the two poles of the AC power supply is shown in Figure 2.
At t = 0, the potential difference between the odd-numbered cylinders and the even-numbered cylinders is positive.
At this moment, an electron is located at the center of a metal plate (numbered 0) connected to the even-numbered cylinders.
The electron starts from rest and is accelerated by the electric field between the plate and cylinder 1, entering cylinder 1 along the central axis.
To ensure that the electron is accelerated in the gaps between the cylinders, the lengths of the cylinders must be designed according to a specific rule.

If the mass of the electron is m, its charge is -e, the voltage of the AC power supply is U, and the period is T, the electric field in the gap between two cylinders can be considered as a uniform electric field, while the electric field inside the cylinders is zero.

targets.text: Figure 1 shows a schematic diagram of an accelerating device, which consists of multiple metal cylinders with the same cross-sectional area arranged in sequence, with their axes aligned on the same straight line.

If the time for electrons to pass through the cylindrical gap cannot be neglected, and the distance of the cylindrical gap is d, while maintaining the same cylindrical length, the variation law of the alternating voltage, and the conditions in (2), what is the maximum speed that the device can allow the electrons to attain?

answer_slots.target_text: If the time for electrons to pass through the cylindrical gap cannot be neglected, and the distance of the cylindrical gap is d, while maintaining the same cylindrical length, the variation law of the alternating voltage, and the conditions in (2), what is the maximum speed that the device can allow the electrons to attain?
answer_slots.expected_answer:
$\sqrt{\frac{2Ue}{m}}$, the electron starts from the circular plate with uniformly accelerated linear motion, enters cylinder 1 and undergoes uniform linear motion, then undergoes uniformly accelerated linear motion in the gap between cylinders 1 and 2, and finally enters cylinder 2 and undergoes uniform linear motion.
$T\sqrt{\frac{nUe}{2m}}$
$\frac{UeT}{2dm}$

sentence_segments.text: When studying the structure of atomic nuclei, it is necessary to bombard the nuclei with particles of very high energy.
To enable charged particles to attain such high energies, scientists have invented various types of particle accelerators.

Figure 1 shows a schematic diagram of an accelerating device, which consists of multiple metal cylinders with the same cross-sectional area arranged in sequence, with their axes aligned on the same straight line.
The cylinders with odd numbers are connected to one pole of an alternating current (AC) power supply, while those with even numbers are connected to the other pole.
The variation pattern of the potential difference between the two poles of the AC power supply is shown in Figure 2.
At t = 0, the potential difference between the odd-numbered cylinders and the even-numbered cylinders is positive.
At this moment, an electron is located at the center of a metal plate (numbered 0) connected to the even-numbered cylinders.
The electron starts from rest and is accelerated by the electric field between the plate and cylinder 1, entering cylinder 1 along the central axis.
To ensure that the electron is accelerated in the gaps between the cylinders, the lengths of the cylinders must be designed according to a specific rule.

If the mass of the electron is m, its charge is -e, the voltage of the AC power supply is U, and the period is T, the electric field in the gap between two cylinders can be considered as a uniform electric field, while the electric field inside the cylinders is zero.
The gravitational force and relativistic effects on the electron are neglected.
Determine the velocity v1 of the electron as it enters cylinder 1, and analyze the motion of the electron from the moment it starts moving from the circular plate until it exits cylinder 2.
If the time for electrons to pass through the gaps between cylinders is neglected, what should be the length Ln of the nth metal cylinder?

If the time for electrons to pass through the cylindrical gap cannot be neglected, and the distance of the cylindrical gap is d, while maintaining the same cylindrical length, the variation law of the alternating voltage, and the conditions in (2), what is the maximum speed that the device can allow the electrons to attain?

targets.text: Find the magnitude and direction of the electric field strength of this uniform electric field.

answer_slots.target_text: If the kinetic energy increment of a particle leaving from point C on the circumference and striking the circumference is $m{v_0}^{2}$, find the initial velocity of the particle when it is incident along the OA direction from point O.
answer_slots.expected_answer:
$\frac{2m{v_0}^{2}}{qR}$, direction along the negative y-axis
$(\frac{2+\sqrt{3}}{2}R,-\frac{R}{2})$ or $(\frac{2-\sqrt{3}}{2}R,-\frac{R}{2})$
$\frac{2\sqrt{2}+\sqrt{6}}{2}v_0$ or $\frac{2\sqrt{2}-\sqrt{6}}{2}v_0$

Subsequently, electron 2 also enters the magnetic field from point $a$ along the direction shown in the figure with the same velocity $v_0$, perpendicular to the magnetic field, and exits the magnetic field at point $c$, the midpoint of the line segment $ab$.
The mass of the electron is $m$, and its charge is $e$.
Find the radius $r_1$ of the motion of electron 1 in the magnetic field.
Find the time $t_1$ for electron 1 to move in the magnetic field.
Find the time $t_2$ for electron 2 to move in the magnetic field.

conditions.text: Within the width indicated by the dashed line, applying a uniform electric field of magnitude $E=2\mathrm{V}/\mathrm{m}$ directed vertically downward can deflect a certain positive ion with an initial velocity (horizontally to the right) of $\mathbf{v}_{0}=1\mathrm{m}/\mathrm{s}$ by an angle of $\theta=30^{\circ}$.
The charge-to-mass ratio of this ion is $\frac{q}{m}=\sqrt{3} \times 10^{4}\mathrm{C/kg}$.

Within the same width, if a uniform magnetic field directed perpendicularly out of the page is applied instead, keeping the initial velocity unchanged, the ion will pass through the region and also be deflected by an angle of $\theta=30^{\circ}$.

targets.text: What is the magnitude of the magnetic induction in a uniform magnetic field?
What are the times for ions to pass through the electric field and the magnetic field, respectively?

answer_slots.target_text: What are the times for ions to pass through the electric field and the magnetic field, respectively?

conditions.text: In the first and fourth quadrants, there is a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the paper and directed inward, with a magnetic induction strength of $B = 5 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{T}$.
The length of the rod is $h = 0.1\,\text{m}$.
The mass of the ball is $m = 1 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg}$, and its charge is $q = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{C}$.
Below the x-axis, there is a barrier PQ (sufficiently long) parallel to the y-axis, with point P located on the x-axis at coordinates $x_p = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{5}\,\text{m}$.

targets.text: When the thin rod moves at a constant velocity $v_0 = 4\,\text{m/s}$ along the positive x-axis under the action of a horizontal external force $F$, determine the motion characteristics of the small ball before it detaches from the rod and the time it takes for the ball to detach from the rod.
In problem 1, determine the work $W$ done by the horizontal external force $F$.
To make the small ball hit the left side of the baffle, determine the range of the uniform velocity of the thin rod moving to the right.

answer_slots.target_text: To make the small ball hit the left side of the baffle, determine the range of the uniform velocity of the thin rod moving to the right.

sentence_segments.text: In a gravity-free space, there is a Cartesian coordinate system xOy.

In the first and fourth quadrants, there is a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the paper and directed inward, with a magnetic induction strength of $B = 5 \times 10^{-2}\,\text{T}$.
A lightweight, insulating, smooth thin rod lies along the y-axis, with its lower end at point O.
The length of the rod is $h = 0.1\,\text{m}$.
At the top of the rod, there is a negatively charged small ball that can slide freely along the rod.
The mass of the ball is $m = 1 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{kg}$, and its charge is $q = 1 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{C}$.
Below the x-axis, there is a barrier PQ (sufficiently long) parallel to the y-axis, with point P located on the x-axis at coordinates $x_p = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{5}\,\text{m}$.
Assume all friction is negligible.

When the thin rod moves at a constant velocity $v_0 = 4\,\text{m/s}$ along the positive x-axis under the action of a horizontal external force $F$, determine the motion characteristics of the small ball before it detaches from the rod and the time it takes for the ball to detach from the rod.
In problem 1, determine the work $W$ done by the horizontal external force $F$.
To make the small ball hit the left side of the baffle, determine the range of the uniform velocity of the thin rod moving to the right.

conditions.text: In a rectangular region (including the boundary), there exists a uniform magnetic field perpendicular to the plane of the paper, with a magnetic induction strength of $B_0 = 2.0 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{T}$.
Points A, B, C, and D are the four vertices of the rectangle, with side BC having a length of $l_1 = 4 \,\mathrm{m}$ and side AB having a length of $l_2 = 2 \,\mathrm{m}$.

A large number of particles with mass $m = 3.2 \times 10^{-26} \,\mathrm{kg}$ and charge $q = -1.6 \times 10^{-18} \,\mathrm{C}$ are injected into the uniform magnetic field from point A along the direction of AD at different velocities.

targets.text: What is the range of values for the particle's speed?
What is the longest duration for a particle to move in the magnetic field?

answer_slots.target_text: What is the longest duration for a particle to move in the magnetic field?
